scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced a standard automotive warranty case only when VIN, coverage, repair-order, authorization, and completion evidence aligned across the named systems of record.
Missing vehicle, coverage, and labor-op prerequisites produced a blocked-state exception instead of improvised warranty handling.
The lane refused closure without technician, claim-line, and parts evidence and preserved an auditable open state.
VIN and causal-part conflicts across DMS, OEM, and parts-retention systems stayed unresolved and were treated as traceability blockers.
Recall and stop-sale boundaries remained intact once the repair order intersected with safety-relevant evidence.
Goodwill and financial override authority was treated as out of scope and routed cleanly.
Repeat-failure and no-trouble-found conditions moved into rework handling with explicit evidence and next-owner routing.
Alternate-part and supplier-quality pressure did not collapse hold or approval boundaries into informal execution authority.
High-volume queue handling preserved realistic automotive terminology and still enforced evidence and sequencing controls.
Missing OEM policy, returned-parts, and recall-ownership facts triggered low-confidence escalation rather than confident synthesis.
Conflicting FTC, OEM, and tenant policy signals were recorded as a precedence conflict that required escalation.
Tenant-specific EV and high-voltage controls were applied without expanding the lane into safety-release or engineering authority.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold with complete ability coverage for the execution-processing-and-fulfillment and manufacturing-and-supply-chain contract.
The package stays inside dealer warranty and product-service execution and does not claim legal, goodwill-approval, supplier-approval, recall-command, or safety-release authority.
